title: Array transformation methods

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Array transformation methods; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Array methods such as map, filter, reduce, find, some, and every encode distinct transformation and query contracts.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: filter retains matching values and map transforms each retained value in a separate step. Your goal is to construct and explain a transformation pipeline and compare it with an explicit loop. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Control flow and functions; pf data selection; Arrays and indexed data; Function declarations, expressions and arrows. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Array methods such as map, filter, reduce, find, some, and every encode distinct transformation and query contracts. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Callbacks can have side effects, reduce needs a sound accumulator, and map does not flatten arbitrary nested arrays. This draft therefore rejects: map should be used for side effects when its returned array is ignored.

includes: Use map, filter, find, some, every and reduce with callbacks whose effects are clear.
Construct and explain a transformation pipeline and compare it with an explicit loop.
excludes: Callbacks can have side effects, reduce needs a sound accumulator, and map does not flatten arbitrary nested arrays.

outcomes: Construct and explain a transformation pipeline and compare it with an explicit loop.

Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Use map when its transformed result is needed; use iteration for deliberate side effects.
